How many of the above statements are correct?

so.t:
● The roots of the League Against Imperialism (LAI) lay in the Second Congress of the Communist International (Comintern) in 1920, which considered the formulation of a colonial policy and included a debate between Lenin and Manabendra Nath Roy, founder of India’s Communist Party.
○ In Brussels in 1927 the LAI was officially founded in the presence of key international political figures, including Jawaharlal Nehru, General Secretary of the Indian National Congress.
● After the Brussels Congress in 1927, the Government of India banned LAI literature from entering India. The All-India Congress Committee recommended in 1927 that the Indian National Congress (INC) seek membership of the LAI, ratified in 1927. So, statement 3 is correct.
○ Controversies over the exact political affiliation of the LAI continued for some years and by 1931 many members had left or been expelled from the League. In April 1930, Jawaharlal Nehru, stung by condemnation of the Delhi Manifesto by members of the LAI, directed Congress to cease all correspondence with the LAI; Nehru was expelled from the LAI the following year. So, statement 1 is correct.
● One of its meetings in 1928 in Limehouse Town Hall protested against the Labour Party’s participation in the Simon Commission and was attended by Tarini Sinha, a member of the Indian Office of the ILP, Sarojini Naidu and Maulana Mohammed Ali, members of the INC. So, statement 2 is correct. So, all three of the above statements are correct.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.

q.t:
Which of the following were the measures taken by the British Colonial Administration to protect Indian industries after the end of World War I in 1918?
1. Appointment of Store purchase committee
2. Appointment of a Fiscal Commission
3. Special protection for cement and petroleum-refining industries
4. System of imperial preference
Select the correct answer using the code given below:

so.t:
● The end of World War-l in 1918 brought into focus the question of the protection of Indian industry. Some remedial measures adopted by the British colonial administration were:
○ In 1919, the Government appointed a Stores Purchase Committee which recommended the need to provide Indian industry with guaranteed minimum orders for government purchases. So, point 1 is correct.
○ The Montagu-Chelmsford Reforms favoured a policy of fiscal autonomy for India. It said that the Secretary of State should avoid interference in fiscal matters.
○ In 1921, the Government appointed a Fiscal Commission under the Chairmanship of Sir Ibrahim Rahimtoola. The commission recommended a policy of protection for industries in the initial stage. It laid down a Triple Formula for industries claiming fiscal protection. So, point 2 is correct.
○ Establishment of a Tariff Board to consider applications for fiscal protection.
○ Protection to steel industry (1924), tinplate industry (1924), paper industry (1925), match industry (1928), cotton textile industry (1930), heavy chemicals industry (1931), sugar industry (1932) and silk yarn and piece-goods industry (1934),
○ Cement, glass and petroleum-refining were denied protection. So, point 3 is not correct.
● The Government of India also accepted the system of Imperial Preferences (consolidated by the Ottawa Imperial Conference of 1932) based on concessional tariffs among members of the British empire. So, point 4 is correct.
○ Imperial preference was a commercial arrangement in which preferential rates (i.e., rates below the general level of an established tariff) were granted to one another by constituent units of an empire.
Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.

opt.t: Reservation in appointments and admission to professional institutions for members of Scheduled Castes
Approval of segregation of religions other than Hinduism into various castes
Prescription of penalties for the enforcement of any disability that arises out of untouchability
Preservation of the inheritance rights over the ancestral property for people who converted to another religion

q.t: Which one of the following clearly states the purpose behind the enactment of the Caste Disabilities Removal Act of 1850?

so.t:
● The Caste Disabilities Removal Act of 1850 was a law passed in British India under East India Company rule, that abolished all laws affecting the rights of people converting to another religion or caste. It was passed to extend the principle of section 9, Regulation VII, 1832, of the Bengal Code throughout India.
● The purpose of this law was to preserve the inheritance rights of the converts in their ancestral property. The new Act allowed Indians who converted from one religion to another religion, equal rights under the new law, especially in the case of inheritance.
● The act provided that laws which inflict on any person forfeiture of rights or property might be held invalid and should cease to be enforced as law.
Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.

opt.t: Election of Pala ruler Gopala by the people to replace an unfit ruler
Position of ministers was made hereditary under the Rashtrakutas
Conversion of Mauryan king Ashoka to Buddhism
Construction of Sun temple by Bhima I of the Solanki dynasty

q.t: In the context of Indian history, Khalimpur copper plate inscription mentions which one of the following events?

so.t:
Pala dynasty (8th century AD - 12th century AD):
● Numerous inscriptions issued by the various Pala rulers gives details about the polity of the dynasty. Some of the important ones are: Khalimpur Copper Plate Inscription issued by Dharmapala (775-810 CE) in the 32nd regnal year after his victory at Kannauj; Badal pillar inscription issued during the reign of king Narayanapala (861-917 CE); and Kamauli Copper Plate Inscription issued during the reign of Kumarapala (1126- 1128 CE).
● The first ruler of this dynasty Gopala (750-775 CE) was elected by the people. The Khalimpur copper
plate inscriptions talk of how King Gopala was elected by the people to replace an unfit ruler.
● According to the Khalimpur inscription, the region was plunged into chaos after the successive attacks
by Yashovarman of Kannauj, Lalitaditya of Kashmir and Harsha of Kamarupa. In order to end the
matsyanyaya or the law of the jungle people had elected Gopala as their ruler.
○ However, his successors generally followed the principles of hereditary succession. The rulers adopted high-sounding titles like maharajadhiraja, parameshvara, parama-bhattaraka etc. The king was perhaps assisted by mantrins and sachivas.
● The Badal pillar inscription indicates that the position of the ministers was hereditary.
Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.

q.t:
Consider the following pairs:

How many pairs given above are correctly matched?

so.t:
● The Butter festival, locally known as Anduri Utsav, is held on the slopes of the alpine meadow on the occasion of Sankranti. It is a festival of expressing gratitude to mother nature. The locals flock with their cattle to the grassland of Dayara Bugyal of Uttarkashi district of Uttarakhand. They do elaborate skits of Lord Krishna. The villagers play ‘Holi’ by applying milk and butter instead of Gulal on each other. So, pair 1 is correctly matched.
● Chingam 1, the first day of the Malayalam calendar. This day marks the beginning of the new harvest season in Kerala. Malayalis perform Pulikali (Tiger Dance). People wear traditional attire, and vibrant flower carpets (Pookkalam) are placed in front of homes. So, pair 2 is not correctly matched.
● Bonalu is a Hindu Festival where Goddess Mahakali is worshipped. It is an annual festival celebrated in the twin Cities Hyderabad and Secunderabad in Andhra Pradesh and other parts of Telangana state. So, pair 3 is not correctly matched.
So, only one of the above pairs is correctly matched.
Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.

opt.t: It required the plantation workers not to leave the tea gardens without permission.
It was primarily concerned with regulating the movement of European settlers within India.
It was enacted to regulate the emigration of Indian labourers to other British colonies.
It sought to abolish the practice of indentured labour in British colonies.

q.t: With reference to the Inland Emigration Act of 1859, which one of the following statements is correct?

so.t: ● Under the Inland Emigration Act of 1859, plantation workers were not permitted to leave the tea gardens without permission and in fact they were rarely given such permission. The British allocated certain places for tea planters to live, but they were restricted to move. Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.

q.t:
Punjab shares its boundary with which of the following?
1. Himachal Pradesh
2. Rajasthan
3. Uttar Pradesh
4. Chandigarh
Correct options:

so.t:
Punjab shares its boundaries with:
Himachal Pradesh - Yes, Punjab shares its northern boundary with Himachal Pradesh.
Rajasthan - Yes, Punjab shares its southern boundary with Rajasthan.
Uttar Pradesh - No, Punjab does not share a boundary with Uttar Pradesh.
Chandigarh - Yes, Chandigarh is a Union Territory surrounded by Punjab, so they share a boundary.

q.t:
Consider the following:
1. Bacteria
2. Virus
3. Fungi
4. Protozoa
How many of the above can cause pneumonia?

so.t:
● Pneumonia is an infection that affects one or both lungs. It causes the air sacs, or alveoli, of the lungs to fill up with fluid or pus.
● Infection with Streptococcus pneumoniae bacteria, also called pneumococcal disease, is the most common cause of pneumonia. Pneumococcal disease can also cause ear infections, sinus infections and meningitis. Mycoplasma pneumoniae bacteria causes atypical pneumonia, which usually has milder symptoms. Other bacteria that cause pneumonia include Haemophilus influenzae, Chlamydia pneumoniae and Legionella (Legionnaires’ disease). So, point 1 is correct.
● Viruses that cause the common cold, the flu (influenza), COVID-19 and respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) can sometimes lead to pneumonia. So, point 2 is correct.
● Fungi, like Cryptococcus, Pneumocystis jirovecii and Coccidioides, are uncommon causes of pneumonia. People with compromised immune systems are most at risk of getting pneumonia from a fungus. So, point 3 is correct.
● Rarely, protozoa like Toxoplasma cause pneumonia. So, point 4 is correct.
So, all four of the above can cause pneumonia.
Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.

q.t:
Consider the following statements:
1. Guru Harkrishan Ji uttered “Baba Bakala” before his death, pointing to the next Guru.
2. Makhan Shah Lubana discovered Guru Tegh Bahadur Ji as the true Guru by offering 200 mohars.

so.t: Both events confirm Guru Tegh Bahadur as the 9th Guru.

q.t:
Consider the following statements with reference to Horizontal Gene Transfer (HGT):
1. It transfers genetic material to its offspring by the sexual movement.
2. Only eukaryotic organisms are capable of undergoing HGT.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?

so.t:
● Horizontal Gene Transfer (HGT), also known as lateral gene transfer is the non-sexual movement of genetic information between genomes. Incoming Deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) or Ribonucleic acid (RNA) can replace existing genes, or can introduce new genes into a genome. It is a process in which an organism transfers genetic material to another organism that is not its offspring. So, statement 1 is not correct.
o Acquisition of DNA through HGT is distinguished from the transmission of genetic material from parents to offspring during reproduction, which is known as vertical gene transfer.
● HGT is known to occur between different species, such as between prokaryotes (organisms whose cells lack a defined nucleus) and eukaryotes (organisms whose cells contain a defined nucleus), and between the three DNA-containing organelles of eukaryotes—the nucleus, the mitochondrion, and the chloroplast. So, statement 2 is not correct.
Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.

q.t:
Consider the following statements with reference to nucleosome:
1. It constitutes the repeating unit of a structure in the nucleus called chromatin.
2. It is a section of Ribonucleic Acid (RNA) that is wrapped around a core of proteins.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?

so.t:
● The nucleosome is the basic repeating sub unit of chromatin packaged inside the cell's nucleus. Inside the nucleus, Deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) forms a complex with proteins called chromatin, which allows the DNA to be condensed into a smaller volume. A nucleosome is a structural unit of DNA packaging in eukaryotes. So, statement 1 is correct.
● The negatively charged DNA (not Ribonucleic Acid (RNA)) is wrapped around the positively charged histone octamer to form a structure called nucleosome. So, statement 2 is not correct.
○ A histone is a protein that provides structural support for a chromosome. Each chromosome contains a long molecule of DNA, which must fit into the cell nucleus. To do that, the DNA wraps around complexes of histone proteins, giving the chromosome a more compact shape.
○ Histones are organised to form a unit of eight molecules called histone octamer.
● When the chromatin is extended and viewed under a microscope, the structure resembles beads on a string. Each of these tiny beads is called a nucleosome and has a diameter of approximately 11 nanometres. Each nucleosome is composed of a little less than two turns of DNA wrapped around a set of eight proteins called histones, which are known as a histone octamer. The chain of nucleosomes is then compacted further and forms a highly organised complex of DNA and protein called a chromosome.
Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.

opt.t: Both statements are true, and Statement II is the correct explanation of Statement I
Both statements are true, but Statement II is not the correct explanation of Statement I
Statement I is true, but Statement II is false
Statement I is false, but Statement II is true

q.t:
Statement I: Punjab shares its international border with Pakistan along a 553 km stretch.
Statement II: The Radcliffe Line forms the international boundary between Punjab and Pakistan.

so.t:
Statement I: Punjab shares its international border with Pakistan along a 553 km stretch.
Correct. Punjab’s international border with Pakistan is approximately 553 km long (official figure by BSF & Govt. of India).
Statement II: The Radcliffe Line forms the international boundary between Punjab and Pakistan.
Correct. The Radcliffe Line (drawn in 1947) demarcates the international boundary between India and Pakistan, and it is the basis for Punjab–Pakistan border.
Now, does Statement II explain Statement I?
• Statement I talks about the length of the border.
• Statement II talks about the origin of the boundary (Radcliffe Line).
• While both are true, the Radcliffe Line doesn’t explain why the border is 553 km in length — it only explains where the boundary comes from.

q.t:
Consider the following:
1. Aspartame
2. Saccharin
3. Stevia
4. Galactose
How many of the above items are non-sugar sweeteners?

so.t:
● Non-sugar sweeteners (NSS) are marketed as low or no-calorie alternatives to free sugars which aid in weight loss and in controlling blood glucose in individuals with diabetes. NSS categories studied by WHO include acesulfame K, aspartame, advantame, cyclamates, neotame, saccharin, sucralose, stevia and stevia derivatives.
● Aspartame is an artificial (chemical) non-sugar sweetener widely used in various food and beverage products since the 1980s, including diet drinks, chewing gum, gelatin, ice cream, dairy products such as yogurt, breakfast cereal, toothpaste and medications such as cough drops and chewable vitamins. So, point 1 is correct.
● Saccharin is a non-nutritive or artificial non-sugar sweetener. ‘Saccharin is used, for instance, to sweeten tea or coffee. It is commonly used as a sugar substitute because it doesn't contain calories or carbs. So, point 2 is correct.
● Stevia is a non-sugar sweetener that can add sweetness to a child's foods and beverages without contributing to calories consumed or added sugars intake. So, point 3 is correct.
● Sugar is one type of carbohydrate, as are fiber and starch. Sugar is an umbrella term for many types of simple carbohydrates, including white table sugar. Also called sucrose, this is the most common sweetener used in sweet desserts and baked goods. Other natural sugars include Fructose, Galactose, Glucose, Lactose, Maltose etc. So, point 4 is not correct.
So, only three of the above items are non-sugar sweeteners.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.

q.t:
With reference to cryogenic rocket engine, consider the following statements:
1. It uses liquid oxygen and liquid hydrogen as propellants.
2. It provides more thrust for propellant it burns compared to solid propellant rocket stages.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?

so.t:
Cryogenic Engine:
● It is a type of rocket engine that uses liquefied gases as fuel and oxidizers. The term cryogenic refers to the fact that these liquefied gases must be kept at cryogenic temperatures, that is, very low temperatures (below -150 degree Centigrade).
● It typically employs liquid oxygen and liquid hydrogen as propellants, instead of conventional rocket fuels. So, statement 1 is correct.
● A cryogenic rocket stage is more efficient and provides more thrust (force) for every kilogram of propellant it burns compared to solid and earth-storable liquid propellant rocket stages. So, statement 2 is correct.
Working of a cryogenic engine:
● As opposed to electric motors, whose power comes from rotating motion, cryogenic engines are reaction engines. In order to work, they throw mass in one direction and rely on the reaction thrust in the opposite direction.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.

opt.t: These are specialised immune cells found in the central nervous system.
These play a significant role in the production of digestive enzymes in the stomach.
These work with neurons to form and store long-term memories.
These are responsible for the production of insulin in the pancreas.

q.t: Which one of the following statements is correct with reference to pericytes?

so.t:
● Neurons, the brain cells responsible for relaying chemical and electrical messages, have long been considered the key players in memory formation. A study found that a type of cell in the blood vessels of the brain collaborates with neurons in the hippocampus (a key region of the brain) to make long-term memories.
○ Found in the walls of tiny blood vessels called capillaries, the collaborators, called pericytes, are crucial for:
■ regulating blood flow in the brain
■ forming blood vessels
■ controlling the entry of immune cells into the central nervous system
■ constituting and maintaining the blood-brain barrier, a thin border of cells that allows only select molecules to move between the brain and blood
■ In addition to these jobs, pericytes also work with neurons to form and store long-term memories.
○ The findings could have implications for research into memory and associated diseases, such as Alzheimer's disease — though more research is needed to further understand the roles of pericytes and the vascular system in memory and its diseases.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.

q.t:
Consider the following:
1. Solving complex mathematical equations
2. Adjudication of legal issues in court
3. Diagnosing medical conditions
4. Automating stock trading
How many of the above are the applications of Artificial Intelligence at the present level of technology?

so.t:
● Artificial Intelligence (AI) leverages computers and machines to mimic the problem-solving and decision making capabilities of the human mind.
● AI can perform complex mathematical calculations and statistical analyses more efficiently than humans. So, point 1 is correct.
● While hearing a dispute between two petitioners, Delhi High Court recently ruled that Artificial Intelligence-based ChatGPT cannot be the substitute for human intelligence and cannot become the basis of adjudication of legal issues in court. At the present stage of technological development, AI cannot substitute either human intelligence or the humane element in the adjudicatory process. At best the tool could be utilised for a preliminary understanding or preliminary research and nothing more. So, point 2 is not correct.
● AI is increasingly used for diagnosing medical conditions through medical imaging analysis and assisting healthcare professionals in making decisions. So, point 3 is correct.
● Designed to optimize stock portfolios, AI-driven high-frequency trading platforms make thousands or even millions of trades per day without human intervention. So, point 4 is correct.
● AI is used in chatbots and virtual assistants to enhance social interaction, but it doesn't necessarily promote emotional intelligence. It is more about simulating human-like interactions. So, only three of the above are the applications of Artificial Intelligence at the present level of technology.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.

opt.t: Both Statement-I and Statement-II are correct and Statement-II is the correct explanation for Statement-I
Both Statement-I and Statement-II are correct and Statement-II is not the correct explanation for Statement-I
Statement-I is correct but Statement-II is incorrect
Statement-I is incorrect but Statement-II is correct

q.t:
Consider the following statements:
Statement-I: The Government of India can be sued in a contract.
Statement-II: The contractual liability of the Government of India is the same as that of an individual.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

so.t:
● Under the exercise of its executive power, the Union or a State can enter into contracts for the acquisition, holding and disposal of property, or to carry on any trade or business, or for any other purpose.
● But, the Constitution of India lays down three conditions which must be fulfilled by such contracts:
○ They must be expressed to be made by the President or Governor, as the case may be.
○ They must be executed on behalf of the President or Governor, as the case may be.
○ They must be executed by such person or in such manner as the President or Governor may direct or authorise.
● These conditions are mandatory and not merely directory in nature. Failure to comply with them nullifies the contracts and renders them void and unenforceable in the courts.
● Further, the President or the Governor is not personally liable in respect of any contract executed in his name. Similarly, the officer executing the contract is also not personally liable. This immunity is purely personal and does not immunize the Government from contractual liability, making the government suable in contracts. So, statement 1 is correct.
● This means that the contractual liability of the Union Government and the State Governments is the same as that of an individual under the ordinary law of contract, which has been the position in India since the days of the East India Company. So, Statement 2 is correct.
So, both Statement-I and Statement-II are correct and Statement-II is the correct explanation for Statement I.
Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.

q.t:
Consider the following statements:
1. Urban Self Governance of the Cantonments falls in the Concurrent List under the Seventh Schedule of the Constitution of India.
2. The Cantonment Board exclusively consists of nominated members.
3. Both Cantonments and Military Stations comprise the military and civil population.
How many of the above statements are correct?

so.t:
● In terms of Entry 3 of the Union List (Schedule VII) of the Constitution of India, Urban Self Governance of the Cantonments and the Housing Accommodation therein is the subject matter of the Union of India. So, statement 1 is not correct.
● The Cantonment Board has equal representation of the elected and nominated/ex-officio members to balance the official representation with the democratic composition. This unique structure of Cantonment Boards is being maintained successfully keeping in view the fact that the Cantonment areas were and are primarily meant to accommodate the military population and their installations. So, statement 2 is not correct.
● Cantonments are different from the Military Stations in that the Military Stations are purely meant for the use and accommodation of the armed forces and these are established under an executive order whereas the Cantonments are areas which comprise both military and civil population. So, statement 3 is not correct. So, none of the above statements are correct.
Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.

q.t:
Consider the following statements with reference to Block Development Officer (BDO):
1. S/He belongs to the State Civil Service cadre.
2. S/He is the ex-officio Secretary of the Panchayat Samiti.
3. S/He has the power to issue certificates regarding Scheduled Castes/Scheduled Tribes and other Backward Classes.
How many of the above statements are correct?

so.t:
The Block is the unit of administration at the lowest level. The officer in-charge of the Block is called Block Development Officer (BDO). She/He belongs to the State Civil Service cadre and looks after various activities of the Block. So, statement 1 is correct.
● The BDO is linked with the middle tier of Panchayati Raj as she/he is the ex-officio Secretary of the Panchayat Samiti and keeps the record of the meetings, prepares budget and coordinates various developmental activities. So, statement 2 is correct.
● Sub-divisional Officer (and not BDO) has the power to issue certificates regarding the domicile, Scheduled Castes/Scheduled Tribes and other Backward Classes. She/He also keeps the land records and collects land revenue. She/He has the power to issue licenses for armed weapons like guns and pistols, and is also authorised for the issuance of Driving License. So, statement 3 is not correct. So, only two of the above statements are correct.
Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.

q.t:
With reference to the National Council of Educational Research and Training (NCERT), consider the following statements:
1. It is an autonomous organisation set up under the Societies Registration Act, 1860.
2. It has been granted Deemed University status by the Union Ministry of Education.
3. It acts as a nodal agency for achieving the goals of universalisation of elementary education.
How many of the above statements are correct?

so.t:
● The National Council of Educational Research and Training (NCERT) is an autonomous organisation set up in 1961 by the Government of India under the Societies Registration Act, 1860. The aim was to assist and advise the Central and State Governments on policies and programmes for qualitative improvement in school education. So, statement 1 is correct.
● NCERT has been given the status of a Deemed University. It has established a formidable presence in research, and actively shaped school education, teacher training and adult literacy. NCERT, on becoming a research university, will offer opportunities for global collaborations and contributions to the global educational landscape. So, statement 2 is correct.
● NCERT organise pre-service and in-service training of teachers; develops and disseminates innovative educational techniques and practices; collaborates and networks with state educational departments, universities, NGOs and other educational institutions; acts as a clearing house for ideas and information in matters related to school education; and act as a nodal agency for achieving the goals of universalisation of elementary education. So, statement 3 is correct. So, all three of the above statements are correct.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.

q.t:
Consider the following statements with reference to the District Mineral Foundation (DMF):
1. It is a non-profit body established under the Mines & Minerals (Development & Regulation) (MMDR) Act, 1957.
2. It aims to work for the interest and benefit of persons and areas affected by mining-related operations.
3. Its composition and functions are prescribed by the State Government.
Which of the statements given above are correct?

so.t:
● District Mineral Foundation (DMF): It has been formed through the amendment in the Mines & Minerals (Development & Regulation) (MMDR) Act, 1957. It is formed in all the districts affected by mining. Accordingly, the MMDR Act provides for the establishment of DMF as a non-profit body. So, statement 1 is correct.
● The objective of the District Mineral Foundation is to work for the interest and benefit of persons and areas affected by mining-related operations in such a manner as may be prescribed by the State Government. So far, DMFs have been set up in 645 districts in 23 States in the country which have framed DMF rules. The composition and functions of the District Mineral Foundation are prescribed by the State Government. So, statements 2 and 3 are correct.
Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.

q.t:
With reference to the United Nations Commission on International Trade Law (UNCITRAL), consider
the following statements:
1. It was established in response to the rapid increase in global trade in the 1960s.
2. It promotes the modernization of international trade laws through the adoption of legislative instruments.
3. It allows foreign professionals and creditors direct access to domestic courts.
How many of the above statements are correct?

so.t:
● The United Nations Commission on International Trade Law is the core legal body of the United Nations system in the field of international trade law. A legal body with universal membership specialising in commercial law reform worldwide for over 50 years, UNCITRAL's business is the modernization and harmonisation of rules on international business.
● The move was in response to the rapid rise of global trade that took place in the 1960s. At the time, national governments recognized the need for harmonised global standards to replace the various national and regional regulations which, until then, largely governed international trade. So, statement 1 is correct.
● UNCITRAL plays a key role in developing a framework in pursuit of its mandate to further the progressive harmonisation and modernization of the law of international trade. UNCITRAL does this by preparing and promoting the use and adoption of legislative and non-legislative instruments in a number of key areas of commercial law. So, statement 2 is correct.
● UNCITRAL allows foreign professionals and creditors direct access to domestic courts and enables them to participate in and commence domestic insolvency proceedings. Besides, it allows recognition of foreign proceedings and enables courts to determine relief accordingly. So, statement 3 is correct.
○ UNCITRAL provides a framework for cooperation between insolvency professionals and courts of countries and allows for coordination in the conduct of concurrent proceedings in different jurisdictions. So, all three of the above statements are correct.

q.t:
Consider the following statements about Nawab Kapur Singh:
1. He organized Sikhs into Budha Dal and Taruna Dal in 1734.
2. He was instrumental in creating Dal Khalsa in 1748.
3. He rejected the Mughal title of Nawab completely.
Correct statement(s):

so.t: Explanation: Title of Nawab was first rejected by the Sikhs but later accepted by Kapur Singh on their behalf.

q.t:
Which statements about the Shivalik Hills are true?
1. Siali Dhar is the highest peak in Punjab.
2. These hills lie in the south-west of Punjab.
3. Podglic soil is found here.
4. Kandi region lies south of the Shivaliks.
Choose the correct options:

so.t:
Siali Dhar is the highest peak in Punjab: The Shivalik Hills, also known as the Siwalik Hills, are a sub-Himalayan range running through Punjab. In Punjab, the highest peak in the Shivalik range is often cited in the Hoshiarpur-Pathankot region. While specific references to "Siali Dhar" as the highest peak are not widely documented, the Shivaliks in Punjab do contain elevated points, and this statement may refer to a local peak. Without contradictory evidence, we can cautiously accept this as potentially true, pending verification of the exact name.
These hills lie in the south-west of Punjab: This is incorrect. The Shivalik Hills are located in the north-eastern part of Punjab, primarily in districts like Hoshiarpur, Gurdaspur, and Pathankot, along the border with Himachal Pradesh. They do not lie in the south-west, which is characterized by flatter, semi-arid regions bordering Rajasthan.
Podglic soil is found here: This seems to be a reference to podzolic soil, commonly found in hilly and forested areas. Podzolic soils are typical in the Shivalik Hills due to the region’s vegetation and climate, which support soil formation under coniferous or mixed forests. This statement is likely true, assuming "Podglic" is a typo for podzolic.
Kandi region lies south of the Shivaliks: This is correct. The Kandi region in Punjab is a submontane zone lying just south of the Shivalik Hills, characterized by undulating terrain, seasonal streams (choes), and less fertile soils. It spans districts like Hoshiarpur and Rupnagar.
Evaluation of Options:
A. 1, 2, and 4: Incorrect because statement 2 is false (Shivaliks are not in south-west Punjab).
B. 1, 3, and 4: Likely correct, as statements 1 (assuming Siali Dhar is a valid reference), 3 (podzolic soil), and 4 (Kandi region) are true or plausible.
C. 1 and 2: Incorrect because statement 2 is false.
D. All of the above: Incorrect because statement 2 is false.

q.t: This tree, scientifically known as Adansonia, is renowned for its unique appearance. Its branches resemble roots sticking up into the sky and have thick trunks. It is native to Africa, Madagascar and Australia. It is known for its drought-resistant qualities and stores water in its trunks, making it well-suited for arid environments. This is the most likely description of:

so.t:
● Baobab is the genus of nine species of deciduous trees of the hibiscus, or mallow, family. Six of the species are endemic to Madagascar, two are native to mainland Africa and the Arabian Peninsula, and one is native to northwestern Australia.
● The Baobab tree is renowned for its unique appearance. Its branches resemble roots sticking up into the sky and hence is also known as the upside-down tree.
● They are known for their drought-resistant qualities and store water in their trunks, making them well-suited for arid environments. Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer. Relevance: The Madhya Pradesh Government has decided that the forest department cannot give permission to translocate Dhar’s famed Baobab trees.

q.t: Which Sufi poet-saint from Punjab wrote in vernacular Punjabi and is famous for his spiritual verses questioning orthodox religious practices?

so.t:
• Bulleh Shah (1680–1757) was a renowned Punjabi Sufi mystic and poet known for his deeply spiritual and humanist poetry written in Punjabi.
• His work challenges rigid religious orthodoxy and ritualism while emphasizing love, tolerance, and personal spiritual experience.
• Bulleh Shah's poetry uses the Kafi verse form and remains celebrated for its lyrical beauty and profound spiritual depth.
• He is one of the most famous Sufi poets of Punjab and is widely recognized for questioning traditional religious practices through his verses.
Hence, option B is correct.

q.t:
With reference to the Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework (GBF), consider the following statements:
1. It is a framework of the Convention of Biological Diversity.
2. It aims for 50 per cent conservation of land, sea and inland waters by 2050.
3. It mandates the elimination of all harmful subsidies by member countries by 2030.
How many of the above statements are correct?

so.t:
● The Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework (GBF) was adopted during the fifteenth meeting of the Conference of the Parties (COP 15) of the Convention of Biological Diversity, following a four-year consultation and negotiation process. It sets out an ambitious pathway to reach the global vision of a world living in harmony with nature by 2050. Among the Framework’s key elements are 4 goals for 2050 and 23 targets for 2030. So, statement 1 is correct.
● Among the twenty-three targets to be achieved by 2030, include 30 per cent conservation of land, sea and inland waters, 30 per cent restoration of degraded ecosystems, halving the introduction of invasive species, and $500 billion/year reduction in harmful subsidies (does not mandate to eliminate all harmful subsidies by 2030). So, statements 2 and 3 are not correct. So, only one of the above statements is correct.
Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.

q.t:
Consider the following with reference to Ethanol:
1. It is commonly used in fire-extinguishers.
2. It is a common ingredient in hand sanitizers.
3. It makes an effective solvent for use in paints.
4. It acts as a preservative in lotions.
5. It is used to oxygenate petrol and reduce air pollution.
How many of the above are applications of ethanol?

so.t:
● Ethanol, also called alcohol, ethyl alcohol and grain alcohol, is a clear, colourless liquid and an ingredient in alcoholic beverages like beer, wine or brandy. It is a natural byproduct of plant fermentation and can be produced through the hydration of ethylene.
Applications of Ethanol:
● Disinfectant: Because ethanol is effective in killing microorganisms like bacteria, fungi and viruses, it is a common ingredient in many hand sanitizers. So, point 2 is correct.
● Household cleaning: Ethanol mixes easily with water and many organic compounds and makes an effective solvent for use in paints, lacquers and varnish, as well as personal care and household cleaning products. So, point 3 is correct. ● Cosmetics: It is a common ingredient in many cosmetics and beauty products. It acts as an astringent to help clean skin, as a preservative in lotions and to help ensure that lotion ingredients do not separate and it helps hairspray adhere to hair. So, point 4 is correct.
● In petroleum products: Petrol contains ethanol, typically in a mixture called E10, made up of 10 percent ethanol and 90 percent petrol/gasoline, to oxygenate the fuel and reduce air pollution. So, point 5 is correct.
● Food industry: As a food additive, ethanol can help evenly distribute food colouring, as well as enhance the flavour of food extracts.
● Ethanol is not used in fire-extinguishers. It is highly flammable and should not be used near open flames.
So, point 1 is not correct.
So, only four of the above are applications of ethanol.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.

q.t:
Zircon, an oldest mineral known to exist on Earth, is used in which of the following?
1. Ceramics
2. Metal casting
3. Refractories
4. Gemstone
5. Cathode Ray Tubes (CRT)
Select the correct answer using the code given below:

so.t:
● Zircon is also referred to as zirconium silicate (ZrSiO4). It is a co-product from the harvesting and processing of ancient heavy mineral sand deposits. It is also the oldest mineral known to exist on Earth. It is the principal source of zirconium. It is widespread as an accessory mineral in felsic igneous rocks.
○ It is a very tough and resistant mineral that has been unaffected by the natural, relatively low temperature processes found on the Earth’s surface.
○ Its chemical reactivity is extremely low, its melting point is well over 2000ºC, its refractive index is very high and is harder than quartz but softer than corundum and diamond.
● The use of micronized zircon and zircon flour in ceramic products accounts for almost half of the worldwide consumption of zircon. It is a common opacifying (an agent added to render something opaque) constituent of glazes applied to ceramic tiles and sanitary ware and is also used as an opacifier in porcelain tiles. So, point 1 is correct.
● Zircon sand is finding increasing use in the metal casting industry because of its good refractory properties, chemical stability, low thermal expansion, excellent bonding properties, high thermal conductivity and resistance to metal penetration. So, point 2 is correct.
● Refractories are materials designed to maintain strength, dimensional stability and chemical inertness over a wide range of temperatures. They are used in high temperature industrial applications such as the handling and containment of molten metals and glass. So, point 3 is correct.
● Zircon is a popular gemstone because it is available in a variety of colours. Hyacinth is the name of gem quality zircon that is yellow-red to red brown and starlite the name given to blue, heat-treated zircon. So, point 4 is correct.
● Cathode Ray Tubes (CRTs) are made from alkali–alumina–silicate glass with a typical thickness. In order to absorb X rays, zirconia derived from the addition of zircon to the glass mixture is included as a constituent of the glass that forms the faceplate portion of the CRT, with the projection tube portion being free of zirconia. So, point 5 is correct.
Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.

opt.t: Indian Chamber of Food and Agriculture
National Agricultural Cooperative Marketing Federation of India (NAFED)
Tribal Co-operative Marketing Development Federation of India (TRIFED)
National Bank for Agriculture and Rural Development (NABARD)

q.t: The ‘Integrated Tribal Development Programme’ is an initiative of which one of the following?

so.t:
● The Integrated Tribal Development Programme is an initiative of the National Bank for Agriculture and Rural Development (NABARD). It aims to improve the livelihoods of 413 tribal families over the next five years by promoting diverse agricultural and non-agricultural activities.
● It follows the Wadi Model of Tribal Development, which focuses on sustainable agriculture, animal husbandry and skill development. The model includes promoting crops like pepper, arecanut and coconut, as well as activities like beekeeping and fish farming, to economically empower tribal communities.
Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.

q.t:
Consider the following statements about Banda Bahadur’s role in Sikh polity:
1. He was the first to establish a Sikh political state.
2. His martyrdom in 1716 marked the beginning of the Misal Age.
3. He introduced the practice of Sarbat Khalsa.
Correct statement(s):

so.t: Explanation: Sarbat Khalsa tradition pre-dated Banda, strengthened later.

q.t: Which one of the following countries is the largest producer of Copper, a major industrial metal critical for the manufacture of solar panels and wind turbines?

so.t:
● Copper (Red Metal) is a major industrial metal with many applications due to its high ductility, malleability and electrical conductivity. Many new technologies critical to fighting climate change, like solar panels and wind turbines, rely on it.
● Chile (South America) is the top copper producer in the world, with 27% of global copper production. In addition, the country is home to the two largest mines in the world, Escondido and Collahuasi. Chile accounted for almost a third of copper mine production & Peru accounted for 10 % of world mine production in 2020.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.

q.t:
Consider the following pairs:

How many of the above pairs are correctly matched?

so.t:
● The Jordan River originates from Mount Hermon close to the boundary between Lebanon and Syria. It is bordered by Jordan in the east; and by Israel and the West Bank of Palestine in the west. It is referred to as Nahr Al Sharieat in Arabic and flows from the north to south direction through the Sea of Galilee to the Dead Sea. So, pair 1 is correctly matched.
● The Yukon River originates from the Llewellyn Glacier in the Canadian province of British Columbia. It flows westwards through Alaska, the United States, and finally empties into the Bering Sea. So, pair 2 is correctly matched.
● The Dnieper River runs from Russia’s Valdai Hills and flows in a southerly direction through Belarus and Ukraine before emptying into the Black Sea. It passes through Russia, Belarus and Ukraine. So, pair 3 is not correctly matched.
● The Danube River flows from the German town of Donaueschingen. The river passes through 10 European nations of Germany, Austria, Hungary, Serbia, Bulgaria, Croatia, Slovakia, Moldova, Ukraine, and Romania and empties into the Black Sea. So, pair 4 is correctly matched. So, only three of the above pairs are correctly matched.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.

q.t:
Which rivers are currently flowing through modern-day Punjab?
1. Jhelum
2. Ravi
3. Beas
4. Sutlej
Select correct options:

so.t:
Modern-day Punjab (Indian Punjab) is traversed by the following rivers from the given options:
Jhelum: The Jhelum River does not flow through Indian Punjab. It primarily flows through Jammu and Kashmir and into Pakistan, forming part of the Indus River system. Therefore, it is not a river of modern-day Indian Punjab.
Ravi: The Ravi River flows through Punjab, particularly in districts like Pathankot and Gurdaspur, before entering Pakistan. This is correct.
Beas: The Beas River flows through Punjab, originating in Himachal Pradesh and joining the Sutlej in Punjab. This is correct.
Sutlej: The Sutlej River is a major river flowing through Punjab, entering from Himachal Pradesh and passing through districts like Rupnagar and Ludhiana before heading into Pakistan. This is correct.
Evaluation of Options:
A. 2, 3, and 4: Correct, as Ravi, Beas, and Sutlej flow through modern-day Punjab, while Jhelum does not.
B. 1, 2, and 3: Incorrect, as Jhelum does not flow through Indian Punjab.
C. All of the above: Incorrect, as Jhelum is not included.
D. 1 and 4 only: Incorrect, as Jhelum does not flow through Punjab, and this option excludes Ravi and Beas, which are correct.

opt.t: Kara sea, Laptev sea, Chukchi sea
Chukchi sea, Laptev sea, Kara sea
Kara sea, Chukchi sea, Laptev sea
Chukchi sea, Kara sea, Laptev sea

q.t: Which one of the following is in the correct order of the following Seas of the Arctic Ocean from west to east?

so.t:
● The Arctic Ocean is the smallest of the world’s oceans. It centres approximately on the North Pole.
● The Arctic Ocean's marginal seas (West to East) are: Chukchi, East Siberian, Laptev, Kara, Barents, Norwegian, Greenland, Lincoln and Beaufort.
So, the correct order (West to East) is: Chukchi sea, Laptev sea, Kara sea.
Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.

q.t: Which Punjabi literary work is considered the first novel in Punjabi literature and deals with social reform themes?

so.t:
"Sundri" (1898) by Bhai Vir Singh is recognized as the first novel in Punjabi literature. It deals with themes of social reform, women's education, and the struggle against social evils like child marriage and caste discrimination. This work marked the beginning of modern Punjabi prose fiction.
About Bhai Vir Singh (1872-1957)
Background:
• Full name: Vir Singh Sandhu
• Born: Amritsar, into a literary family
• Education: Studied at Amritsar and Lahore
• Movement: Key figure in the Singh Sabha movement
• Legacy: Known as the "Father of Modern Punjabi Literature"
"Sundri" (1898) - The Pioneering Novel
Details:
• Year: 1898
• Publisher: Initially serialized in the journal "Khalsa Samachar"
• Length: Substantial work, establishing the novel form in Punjabi
• Language: Written in accessible Punjabi prose

q.t:
In India, Gift tax is levied on which of the following transactions?
1. Gifts received during weddings
2. Gifts received as part of the inheritance
3. Gifts whose value exceeds Rs.50,000
4. Acquisition of equity shares in Public Sector Units (PSUs) through strategic disinvestment
Select the correct answer using the code given below:

so.t:
● The Government of India implemented the Gift Tax Act, 1958 (GTA) with the purpose of levying taxes on the exchange of gifts in specific situations. It encompassed gifts in various forms, including cash, demand drafts, bank cheques, or any items of value.
● According to the Income Tax Act, gifts exceeding Rs. 50,000 in value are subject to gift tax for the recipient. Certain transfers that may not be considered traditional gifts can also be subject to gift tax. So, point 3 is correct.
● Exemptions from gift tax are granted for gifts from relatives, which include parents, spouses, siblings, spouse's siblings, and lineal descendants of the spouse.
● Situations where gifts can be exempted from tax are:
○ Gifts received during the wedding. So, point 1 is not correct.
○ Gifts received as part of an inheritance. So, point 2 is not correct.
○ Cash or rewards received as recognition of merit by local authorities or educational institutions are also exempt from gift tax.
● Recently, the Central Board of Direct Taxes (CBDT) has also exempted buyers from gift tax when they acquire equity shares in Public-Sector Units (PSUs) through strategic disinvestment. So, point 4 is not correct. Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.

q.t:
Consider the following:
1. Increase in investment demand
2. Increase in profit of a company
3. Increase in total output of goods at lower capital cost
How many of the above are the possible consequences of a decrease in the Marginal Product of Capital?

so.t:
● Marginal Product of Capital refers to the change in the output produced by the company when an additional unit of capital is employed. At the same time, the other inputs are constant.
● It plays an important role for the company’s management because the decisions for the different investments in the company are taken after comparing the marginal product of the capital arrived with the respective cost of capital.
● The formula for the calculation of the marginal product of capital is as follows:
○ Marginal Product of Capital (MPK) = Change in Total Output / Change in Capital; where,
■ Change in Total Output = Change in the units produced by the company which is calculated by subtracting the level of old production from the level of the new production units.
■ Change in Capital = Change in the capital of the company which is calculated by subtracting the previous amount of capital from the new amount of the capital.
● The marginal product of capital is the additional output resulting, keeping all other factors constant, from the use of an additional unit of physical capital, such as machines or buildings used by businesses. As the amount of capital increases, MPK decreases and can eventually become negative. This is the scenario where adding an additional unit of physical capital can actually decrease production instead of increasing it. So, point 3 is not correct.
● The decline in the marginal product of capital leads to a reduction in investment demand, causing the capital market to clear at a lower real interest rate. So, point 1 is not correct.
● An increase in the marginal product of capital (not a decrease in the marginal product of capital) as the cost of capital remains constant provides an ideal operating situation, which can allow a firm to generate a substantial amount of profits. Measuring the marginal productivity of capital thus allows firms to maximise their output and profit.
So, point 2 is not correct.

q.t: ‘‘Resilience and Sustainability Facility (RSF)’’ is related to the provisions of lending by which one of the following?

so.t:
● The International Monetary Fund (IMF) is an international organisation that promotes sustainable growth and prosperity for all of its 190 member countries. It was established in 1944 in the aftermath of the Great Depression of the 1930s.
● The IMF has several lending instruments to meet the different needs and specific circumstances of its members.
● The Resilience and Sustainability Facility (RSF) provides affordable long-term financing to countries undertaking reforms to reduce risks to prospective balance of payments stability, including those related to climate change and pandemic preparedness.
Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.

opt.t: It is a DNA sequence that moves from one location of the genome to another.
It is a plant hormone that promotes plant growth and development.
It is a cell organelle that processes and packages proteins and lipids.
It is a cell organelle that contains digestive enzymes to break down excess cell parts.

q.t: What is a ‘Transposon’?

so.t:
● Transposons: They are also known as jumping genes. They are sequences of DNA that can move or "jump" to different parts of the genome. They are used in genetic editing and therapy due to their ability to alter gene expression and introduce genetic changes. Researchers have harnessed these properties to develop tools for gene therapy and genetic correction.
● They influence the effects of genes by turning ‘on’ or ‘off’ their expression using a variety of epigenetic mechanisms. They are thus rightly called the tools of evolution, for their ability to rearrange the genome and introduce changes.
● More than 45% of the human genome consists of transposable elements. Just as they create diversity, they also create mutations in genes and lead to diseases. However, most of the transposons have themselves inherited mutations and have become inactive, and thus can’t move around within the gnome.
● Over the years, researchers have attempted to resurrect inactive transposons from the genomes of the animal kingdom, hoping that the results will be useful in biomedical applications like genetic correction to cure a disease or for gene therapy.
Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.

q.t:
Consider the following:
1. Digital Aadhaar
2 Unified Payment Interface (UPI)
3. Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP)
4. Short Message Service (SMS)
5. Simple Mail Transfer Protocol (SMTP)
How many of the above are examples of Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI)?

so.t:
● Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) is a set of technology building blocks that drive innovation, inclusion, and competition at scale, operating under open, transparent and participatory governance that ensures global information exchange and interoperability. Following are the examples of DPI:
○ Digital Aadhaar. So, point 1 is correct.
○ Unified Payment Interface (UPI). So, point 2 is correct.
○ The Internet is powered by common protocols like Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP), Hypertext Markup Language (HTML), and Simple Mail Transfer Protocol (SMTP). So, points 3 and 5 are correct.
○ Telecom, with standards like Global System for Mobile (GSM), Short Message Service (SMS) and Code-division Multiple Access (CDMA). So, point 4 is correct.
So, all five of the above are examples of Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI).
Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.

opt.t: It was primarily a religious reform movement without political implications
It aimed to gain control of gurdwaras from British-appointed mahants and had significant political undertones
It was launched exclusively by urban educated Sikhs
It had no connection with the broader Indian independence movement

q.t: Regarding the nationalist movement in Punjab, which statement is most accurate about the Akali movement?

so.t:
The Akali movement (1920s) was indeed focused on gaining control of gurdwaras from mahants (priests) who were often corrupt and had British support. However, it had significant political implications as it challenged British authority and connected with the broader independence movement. It involved both rural and urban participants and was part of the non-cooperation movement.
• Option A is incorrect because the movement was more than religious reform; it had strong political aspects.
• Option C is incorrect as the movement had wide base participation including rural Sikhs, not exclusively urban educated Sikhs.
• Option D is incorrect because the Akali movement was interconnected with the larger Indian independence movement.
Historical Background and Context
Timeline and Origins (1920-1925)
• Peak Period: 1920-1925, coinciding with Gandhi's Non-Cooperation Movement
• Founding: Shiromani Akali Dal formed in 1920
• Catalyst: Nankana Sahib massacre (February 20, 1921) where mahants killed innocent Sikh pilgrims
The Mahant System Problem
What were Mahants?
• Definition: Hereditary priests who controlled gurdwaras
• British Support: Colonial government supported them because:
o They were politically compliant
o Provided stability to British rule
o Often corrupt and could be manipulated
• Issues with Mahants:
o Many were corrupt and immoral
o Misused gurdwara funds and property
o Some didn't follow Sikh religious practices
o Turned sacred places into personal fiefdoms
Key Campaigns:
• Nankana Sahib (1921): Most famous struggle where mahants massacred peaceful protesters
• Guru ka Bagh (1922): Peaceful satyagraha lasting 6 months
• Jaito (1923-24): Resistance against the ruler of Nabha state
• Golden Temple (1921): Removing corrupt mahants from Harmandir Sahib

q.t:
Consider the following:
1. Public Provident Fund
2. Post Office Monthly Income Scheme
3. Sovereign Gold Bonds
4. Kisan Vikas Patra
How many of the above are classified as Small Savings Instruments in India ?

so.t:
● Small Savings Instruments (SSIs) are a set of savings instruments managed by the Central Government to encourage citizens to save regularly, irrespective of their age. They provide returns that are generally higher than bank fixed deposits. It also gives a sovereign guarantee and tax benefits. The interest rates on small savings schemes change every quarter.
● All deposits received under various small savings schemes are pooled in the National Small Savings Fund. The money in the fund is used by the Central Government to finance its fiscal deficit.
● These instruments can be classified under three headings:
○ Social security schemes (Public Provident Fund (PPF) and Senior Citizens’ Savings Scheme (SCSS). So, point 1 is correct.
○ Postal deposits (comprising savings accounts, recurring deposits, time deposits of varying maturities and Monthly Income Scheme (MIS). So, point 2 is correct.
○ Savings certificates (National Small Savings Certificate (NSC) and Kisan Vikas Patra (KVP). So, point 4 is correct.
● Sovereign Gold Bonds (SGBs) are government securities denominated in grams of gold. They are substitutes for holding physical gold. They are issued by the Reserve Bank of India (RBI) on behalf of the Government of India. They do not come under the category of SSIs. So, point 3 is not correct. So, only three of the above are classified as Small Savings Instruments.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.

opt.t: The successful encirclement of the German 6th Army through Operation Uranus, cutting off supplies and reinforcements
The premature onset of winter weather, which paralyzed German mechanized units before capturing the city
American Lend-Lease military equipment arriving in sufficient quantities to equip Soviet defenders
The diversion of German forces to defend against the Allied invasion of North Africa

q.t: The Battle of Stalingrad (1942-43) is considered the decisive turning point on the Eastern Front. Which strategic factor was MOST critical to the Soviet victory?

so.t: Explanation: Operation Uranus, launched on November 19, 1942, was the Soviet counter-offensive that encircled the German 6th Army under General Friedrich Paulus in Stalingrad. Soviet generals Zhukov and Vasilevsky meticulously planned this double envelopment, attacking the weaker Romanian forces protecting the German flanks north and south of the city. Within five days, approximately 250,000-300,000 Axis troops were trapped in the Stalingrad pocket. This encirclement proved decisive because it cut off German supply lines, preventing resupply by air (the Luftwaffe could not deliver sufficient supplies) and blocking any ground relief efforts, including Operation Winter Storm. The trapped forces gradually starved and froze, ultimately surrendering in February 1943. Option B is incorrect regarding timing—the battle began in August 1942, and while winter conditions were harsh, they affected both sides, and the encirclement (not weather) was the decisive factor. Option C overstates the immediate impact of Lend-Lease at Stalingrad; while important to the overall Soviet war effort, the victory primarily relied on Soviet production and military planning. Option D is misleading—Operation Torch (North Africa invasion) occurred in November 1942 but did not significantly divert forces from Stalingrad, as German priorities remained focused on the Eastern Front.

q.t:
Consider the following statements with reference to Clearing Corporations in India:
1. They approve the establishment of Social Stock Exchanges in India.
2. They act as a mediator between entities engaged in a financial transaction.
3. They set the price and maturity of the contract for entities engaged in a financial transaction.
How many of the above statements are correct?

so.t:
● A Clearing Corporation (CC) is an organisation/entity affiliated with a stock exchange whose primary objective is to oversee the handling of confirmation, settlement, and delivery of transactions. They are also referred to as Clearing Firm or Clearing House.
● A CC acts as a mediator between any two entities or parties that are engaged in a financial transaction. Its main role is to ensure that the transaction goes smoothly, with the buyer receiving the tradable goods he intends to acquire and the seller receiving the right amount paid for the tradable goods he is selling. This is done by checking the financial capabilities of both parties to enter into a legal transaction, regardless of whether they are an individual or an organization. So, statement 2 is correct.
○ The CC makes sure that the parties involved respect the system and follow the proper procedures for a successful transaction. The facilitation of smooth transactions leads to a more liquid market.
○ It is the CC that provides a level playing field for both parties, where they can agree on the terms of their negotiation. This includes having the responsibility for setting the price, quality, quantity and maturity of the contract. So, statement 3 is correct.
○ The CC makes sure that the right goods are delivered to the buyer, in terms of both quantity and quality, so that at the end of the transaction there are no complaints nor arbitration necessary.
● Approval and supervision of stock exchanges in India is a function of the Securities and Exchange Board of India (SEBI). So, statement 1 is not correct.
So, only two of the above statements are correct.
Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.

q.t: ‘Pearl Spot’ and ‘Moon wrasse’, recently seen in the news, are:

so.t:
● Pearl Spot (Karimeen): It is a species of cichlid fish found in southern India. It is commonly known as “Karimeen” in Kerala and extensively found along the east and south-west coasts of Peninsular India. It is one of the most widely available varieties in the South, especially in the Kerala backwaters, Karnataka and Andhra Pradesh. It is listed as Least Concern on the IUCN Red List of Threatened Species.
● Moon wrasse: It is a fish with vertical red lines on the body scales and a crescent moon-shaped yellow patch on the caudal fin. The species is found in tropical and warm temperate waters of the Indo-Pacific. It is listed as Least Concern on the IUCN Red List of Threatened Species.
Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.

q.t:
Consider the following pairs:

How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?

so.t:
● Pulakesin I (540–567 CE) gained independence at the decline of the Satavahana Empire and established the Chalukya Dynasty. The Chalukya emperors and their successors reigned over the vast Deccan plateau for nearly 500 years.
● A royal Indian dynasty, the Chalukyas ruled over large parts of south and central India between the 6th and 8th century AD. They ruled as three individual dynasties:
○ The Western Chalukyas were the ones who ruled from Vatapi (modern Badami). So, pair 1 is not correctly matched.
○ Eastern Chalukyas were the ones who ruled from Vengi. So, pair 2 is correctly matched.
○ Later Western Chalukyas were the ones who ruled from Kalyani. So, pair 3 is not correctly matched.
So, only one of the above pairs is correctly matched.
Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.

q.t:
With reference to the Vijayanagara empire, consider the following statements:
1. Palayakkarargal (poligar) was the holder of a territory granted to him in return for military service and tribute.
2. The Palayakkarar system was first introduced in India during this period.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?

so.t:
● In South India, poligars (palayakkarargal) were the holders of land territory or palayam consisting of a few villages, granted to them by the Nayaka ruler in return for military service and tribute. So, statement 1 is correct.
● The Palayakkarar system was a significant administrative and military structure system. It was initially practised during Prataba Rudhra's reign in the Kakatiya kingdom before being introduced in Tamil Nadu. It emerged during the Vijayanagara Empire's rule in Tamil Nadu and other parts of South India. It is attributed to Viswanatha Nayaka, who established this system in Tamil Nadu upon assuming the role of the Nayak ruler of Madurai in 1529, aided by his minister, Ariyanathar. So, statement 2 is not correct
. ● They revolted against the British from 1795-1805 in centres like Tirunelveli, Ramanathapuram, Sivaganga, Madurai and North Arcot. The origins of this conflict can be traced back to 1781 when the Nawab of Arcot ceded the administration and authority of Tinneveli and the Carnatic Provinces to the East India Company.
Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer

opt.t: To facilitate gathering for discussion on philosophy and experience
To honour the new members who followed Lingayatism
To compile the literary works authored by leading gurus
To arrange and systematise rules and codes of the Lingayat sect

q.t: Anubhava Mantapa was organised by Guru Basavanna mainly for which one of the following reasons?

so.t:
● Anubhava Mantapa is considered as the first parliament in the history of mankind. It was an assembly organised by Guru Basavanna in the city of Kalyana (Karnataka) to facilitate gathering together for philosophy and experience.
● Prabhudeva, a great Yogi of extraordinary achievement, was the President and Lord Basava acted as the Prime Minister. Chennabasava can be compared to the Speaker while at the same time working as the editor and compiler of Vachana literature.
Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.

q.t:
Consider the following statements regarding the People’s Biodiversity Register (PBR):
1. It is prepared by the respective State Biodiversity Boards of each State.
2. It includes details of the observed impacts of climate change on biodiversity.
3. Maharashtra is the first State to complete the PBR for each local self-government body.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?

so.t:
● The Biological Diversity Act of 2002 requires local bodies, such as Panchayats, Municipalities and other local governance structures, to prepare People’s Biodiversity Registers (PBRs). These registers document the biological resources and traditional knowledge within their jurisdictions.
○ PBRs are primarily prepared by local bodies with support from Biodiversity Management Committees (BMCs) at the local level. State-level biodiversity boards may provide guidance and oversight, but the actual preparation is a grassroots effort. So, statement 1 is not correct.
● PBRs encompass a wide range of information, including the documentation of traditional knowledge held by local communities and the observed impacts of climate change on biodiversity. This ensures a comprehensive understanding of the region’s biological diversity. So, statement 2 is correct.
● Kerala has become the first state in India to complete the preparation of PBR for each local self government (LSG) body, ahead of the National Green Tribunal's deadline of January 31, 2020. This demonstrates the state’s commitment to documenting and preserving its rich biodiversity. So, statement 3 is not correct. Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.

q.t: Who among the following set up the Adalat-I Ala in Lahore for the administration of justice?

so.t:
● Maharaja Ranjit Singh (born in 1780) was the founder and maharaja (1801–39) of the Sikh kingdom of the Punjab. ● During Ranjit Singh's reign, justice was administered by the Panchayats in the villages and the Kardars in the towns. In the provincial headquarters, there were the courts of the Nazims. Above them, there was the Adalat-i-Ala (Supreme Court) in Lahore. The final court of appeal was the Maharaja himself.
Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.

q.t: 'Suka Saptati', written by Chintamani, is believed to be the first book to have been translated into Persian. It was translated by:

so.t:
● The first Sanskrit book which is believed to have been translated into Persian was Suka-Saptati.
○ Suka Saptati was written by Chintamani and was translated into Persian by Sufi author Zia' al Din Nakshabi.
○ The Tutinama (Tales of a Parrot) is a literary work written in CE 1329-30.
○ The paintings of the Tuti-nama depict scenes from the 52 stories told on successive nights by a pet parrot to beguile Khujasta, the wife of his owner, through the night so that she would be too enthralled to leave home to meet her lover and succumb to an adulterous affair.
Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.

q.t:
Consider the following:
1. Pavapuri
2. Lalitgiri
3. Ratnagiri
4. Udaygiri
Which of the above Buddhist Heritage Sites are part of the Diamond Triangle of Odisha?

so.t:
● The Diamond Triangle of Odisha consists of three Buddhist sites namely, Ratnagiri, Udaygiri and Lalitgiri. Odisha was the cradle of Vajrayana or Tantric or esoteric Buddhism. This sect of Buddhism is also known as the Diamond Vehicle and so comes the name Diamond Triangle. The three sites are located in the Jajpur district of Odisha. So, points 2, 3 and 4 are correct.
○ Lalitagiri (also known as Nalitagiri): Its complex is home to stupas, 'esoteric' Buddha images, and monasteries (viharas), which is the oldest site in the region. Significant finds at this complex include Buddha's relics.
○ Ratnagiri (meaning "hill of jewels"): It is the site of a ruined mahavihara, once the major Buddhist monastery in Odisha. It is located on a hill in between the Brahmani and Birupa rivers in Jajpur district. Monastery 1 located here has been described as "the finest in terms of carved stone decoration to have survived in India".
○ Udaygiri: Its complex of Madhavpura Mahavihara comprises of Mahastupa made up of burnt bricks with Dhyani Buddha Akshobhya on the Eastern side, Amitabha on the Western side, Amoghasiddhi on the North and Ratnasambhava on the South. The shrine chamber houses the statue of Buddha in Bhumisparsha mudra.
○ Pawapuri or Pavapuri (also called Apapapuri, meaning "the sinless town"): It is a holy site for Jains located in the Nalanda district of Bihar. It is the place of Mahavira's nirvana and a pilgrimage site for Jains. So, point 1 is not correct.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.

q.t:
Consider the following statements with reference to the African Swine Fever (ASF):
1. It is caused by a virus.
2. It is a zoonotic disease that can spread to humans.
3. It has been declared as a global health emergency by the World Health Organisation (WHO).
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?

so.t:
● African Swine Fever (ASF) is caused by the African Swine Fever virus (ASFV), which affects domestic and wild pigs. The virus leads to severe hemorrhagic fever with high mortality rates in pigs. So, statement 1 is correct.
○ It is not a zoonotic disease and does not spread to humans. It exclusively affects pigs and does not pose a direct health risk to humans. So, statement 2 is not correct.
● Currently, there is no effective vaccine available for ASF. Efforts are ongoing to develop a vaccine, but as of now, prevention relies primarily on biosecurity measures to control the spread of the virus. It has not been declared as a global health emergency by the World Health Organisation (WHO). So, statement 3 is not correct.
Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.

opt.t: neutralisation of cancer cells through modification in genes
DNA amplification through Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR)

q.t: Barnase/barstar system is associated with:

so.t:
● The barnase/barstar system is a genetically engineered system that provides an efficient and robust alternative method for hybrid seed production in mustard. It is an indelible technique to produce hybrid seeds in self-pollinated crops.
● It has been successfully deployed in countries like Canada, Australia and America for many decades.
○ The barnase gene codes for a protein that impairs pollen production and renders the plant into which it is incorporated male-sterile.
○ This plant is then crossed with a fertile parental line containing the barstar gene that blocks the action of the barnase gene.
○ The resultant F1 progeny is both high-yielding and also capable of producing seed/ grain.
● In India, the Centre for Genetic Manipulation of Crop Plants (CGMCP), University of Delhi South Campus, New Delhi has made a successful attempt with some alterations in the barnase/ barstar system which culminated in the development of GM mustard hybrid MH11.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.

q.t:
Consider the following:
1. Processed meat
2. Arecanut
3. Benzene
4. Asbestos
How many of the above are characterised as carcinogens by the World Health Organisation (WHO)?

so.t:
● The International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC) under the World Health Organisation (WHO) classifies items into different categories based on their potential risk of causing cancers.
● Group 1: "Carcinogenic to humans" - includes items for which there is enough evidence to conclude that it can cause cancer in humans.
o Some of the items included under this list include: asbestos, alcohol, radiation, benzene, aflatoxins, acetaldehyde (from consuming alcoholic beverages), arecanut, tobacco, chronic infection with hepatitis B & C viruses, consumption of processed meat, untreated or mildly treated mineral oils, etc. So, points 1, 2, 3 and 4 are correct.
So, all four of the above are characterised as carcinogens by the World Health Organisation (WHO).
Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.

q.t:
Consider the following statements regarding the Agnipath scheme:
1. It is open for both male and female candidates between the age of 171/2 to 21 years.
2. It provides Agniveers with a pension and medical facilities post-retirement.
3. The maximum period of service for Agniveers is ten years.
How many of the above statements are correct?

so.t:
Explanation: Agniveer scheme: Its aim is to give young people the chance to experience military life without committing to a long-term career. It also aims to increase the Armed Forces' war readiness and to teach young people military principles and skills that they can apply in civil society. Candidates inducted through this scheme will be called Agniveers.
● The age group for recruitment into the Agniveer scheme is typically between 17.5 and 21 years, not extending up to 25 years. This age range is designed to attract young individuals for a short-term service period. So, statement 1 is correct.
● Agniveers are not entitled to a pension post-retirement. Instead, they receive a one-time 'Seva Nidhi' package at the end of their four-year service period. They do, however, receive medical benefits during their service. So, statement 2 is not correct.
● The scheme mandates a service period of four years. During this time, Agniveers undergo training and serve in various capacities within the armed forces. So, statement 3 is not correct.
● It includes a compensation package for the families of Agniveers who die in the line of duty consisting of an ex gratia payment and other benefits. This comprehensive package amounts to approximately Rs 1.65 crore, aimed at providing financial support to the bereaved families.
So, only one of the above statements is correct.
Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.

q.t:
Consider the following statements with reference to Biofilms:
1. They are 3D structures of multiple microorganisms attached to the surface.
2. Mycobacterium tuberculosis forms biofilms that host drug-tolerant bacteria.
3. They can include bacteria, fungi and protists.
How many of the above statements are
correct?

so.t:
● Biofilms are 3D structures of multiple microorganisms (different bacterial colonies or single types of cells in a group), attached to the surface. So, statement 1 is correct.
● Recent studies suggested that Mycobacterium tuberculosis forms biofilms that host drug-tolerant bacteria. Studies found that cellulose is a key component of these biofilms, holding mycobacterial cells together in these biofilms. So, statement 2 is correct.
○ These cells are embedded in extracellular polymeric substances, a matrix which is generally composed of eDNA, proteins and polysaccharides, showing high resistance to antibiotics. It is one of the major causes of infection persistence especially in nosocomial settings through indwelling devices.
● Biofilms can include bacteria, fungi and protists. So, statement 3 is correct.
○ Examples of biofilms include: Dental plaque, Pond scum.
So, all three of the above statements are correct.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.

opt.t: These are exotic particles that are their own antiparticles.
These particles can only exist at extremely low temperatures close to absolute zero.
These are predicted to be components of dark matter in the universe.
These are elementary particles that possess both electric and magnetic charges.

q.t: Which of the following statements is correct with reference to ‘Majorana fermions’?

so.t:
● Majorana fermions are exotic particles that have generated significant interest in the field of quantum computing. These particles, first proposed by Ettore Majorana, possess a unique property: they are their own antiparticles. This characteristic makes them highly stable and less susceptible to external influences, a crucial feature in quantum computing, where maintaining quantum coherence is paramount.
● Majorana fermions have the potential to serve as qubits in topological quantum computers. Their topological properties protect against errors, which are a significant challenge in building practical quantum computers. Harnessing Majorana fermions could lead to more robust and efficient quantum computing systems, with applications in solving complex problems and cryptography. Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.

q.t:
Consider the following statements with reference to the Unani system of medicine:
1. It is an indigenous system that originated in the Vedic Period in India.
2. Its drugs are formulated using only plant components.
3. It is based on the four humours balance theory.
How many of the above statements are correct?

so.t:
● Unani system of medicine is a comprehensive medical system, which provides preventive, promotive, curative and rehabilitative health care.
● Originating in Greece (Yunan), this system, after getting further developed in the Arab and Persian lands, came to India and took deep roots in Indian civilization. So, statement 1 is not correct.
